item.title: Topolski's Chronicle, V.5 No.9, 1957
item.creator: Topolski, Feliks, 1907-1989
item.description: Topolski's Chronicle is a series of broadsheets created by Feliks Topolski and distributed bimonthly to subscribers from 1953 through 1982. Topolski was a superlative draughtsman and covered current events with an artistic, unique style.

pages.typeset_text: JOYCE CARY, the blessed writer-impersonator, writer - 'saviour', died at his home in Oxford on March 29th of this year. Having created his sinners, he miraculously entered them and, becoming Sarah the trollop-cook, Gulley Jimson the 'gipsy' artist, Wilcher the exhibitionist miser, earned their redemption in our eyes.
sketch_descriptions: A detailed expressionistic black ink sketch of an elderly man's head and shoulders, facing left, with pronounced facial lines and a thoughtful smile, drawn in a lively cross-hatched style on brownish paper.
pages.page_summary: This page features a portrait sketch of writer Joyce Cary by Felix Topolski, accompanied by a text eulogy marking Cary's death on March 29, 1957, and highlighting his literary characters.

pages.typeset_text:
POLAND (AND ALL EUROPE) IS GUTTED OUT OF ITS BLACK JEWISH FOLKLORE. IN MY POLISH YOUTH I WENT, INCURIOUS,
BY THAT WORLD - NOW IRRETRIEVABLE. THEREFORE THE FLEETING MAKE-BELIEF RESURRECTION OF IT, BROUGHT TO
LONDON BY THE POLISH STATE JEWISH THEATRE, IS RECORDED HERE NOT AS THEATRE BUT AS REMEMBERED REALITY.
sketch_descriptions: Top row features sketches of several Jewish men mostly viewed from behind or side, wearing traditional hats and coats, some with beards.
Middle left shows a group of three Jewish men sitting closely at a table, engaged in discussion or study.
Middle right depicts five men in a semi-circle, possibly in conversation or study, two viewed from behind and three facing the viewer.
Bottom left details a group of four individuals including an old man with a beard wearing a tall hat, a woman with long hair, and two other figures.
Bottom right shows a gathering of six people clustered together, some seated, one man reading a book, with elements of traditional Jewish dress including hats and head coverings.
pages.page_summary: This page reflects the artist's nostalgic remembrance and depiction of Jewish folklore and cultural life from Poland and Europe, highlighted by sketches and a textual reflection on its loss and theatrical preservation in London.
